summary: American Transcendentalist writer. Best known for Walden (account of two years living near a Massachusetts pond) and "Civil Disobedience" (essay about refusing to pay poll-tax and spending a night in jail). Close friendship with Ralph Waldo Emerson, on whose land the Walden cabin was built. Also wrote A Week on the Concord and Merrimack Rivers and defended John Brown.

works.name: Walden
works.description: Account of two years, two months, and two days spent living in a cabin near Walden Pond, on Emerson's land. Chapters include "Economy" and "Higher Laws."

chapters of which are titled 'Economy' and 'Higher Laws'

works.name: Civil Disobedience
works.description: Essay (originally "Resistance to Civil Government") about refusing to pay the poll-tax and spending a night in jail. Argued the best government "governs least."

clues.clue: Describes being put in jail for refusing to pay poll-tax — cut short after single night due to anonymous relative's intervention
examples: described being put in jail for refusing to pay his taxes

examples: harangues 'merchants and farmers' in Massachusetts who 'are not prepared to do justice to the slave and to Mexico'

works.name: A Week on the Concord and Merrimack Rivers
works.description: Account of a boat trip taken with his brother John, who later died of tetanus.

clues.clue: Recalls a boat trip with brother John who died of tetanus — author was "profoundly affected"
examples: was profoundly affected by the death of his brother John from tetanus, recalling a boat trip that he took with him

comprehensive_summary:
Henry David Thoreau is an American Transcendentalist writer best known for Walden, an account of two years, two months, and two days spent living in a cabin near Walden Pond, built on Ralph Waldo Emerson's land. The book's author "wished to live deliberately, to front only the essential facts of life." Its section titled "Economy" provides an account of the cost of building the cabin, and "Brute Neighbors" contains the famous "Battle of the Ants" between "red imperialists" and "black republicans." The book was inspired by Emerson's call for "solitude" in his essay "Nature." Annie Dillard's Pilgrim at Tinker Creek is often thematically compared to Walden, and Dillard's MA thesis was on the book.

"Civil Disobedience" (originally "Resistance to Civil Government") describes Thoreau's night in jail for refusing to pay the poll-tax, cut short by an anonymous relative's intervention. The essay opens by accepting the motto "the best government is that which governs least" (borrowed from O'Sullivan's Democratic Review). The experience makes him realize his neighbors' "friendship was for summer weather only" and leads him to call the State "timid as a lone woman with her silver spoons." The essay harangues "merchants and farmers" about slavery and Mexico. It was fictionalized by Robert E. Lee and Jerome Lawrence in The Night Thoreau Spent in Jail.

Thoreau was profoundly affected by the death of his brother John from tetanus and recalled their boat trip in A Week on the Concord and Merrimack Rivers. He spent three years as a tutor to Emerson's children and met Emerson at Harvard in 1837. Emerson's eulogy lamented that "instead of engineering for his entire country, he was the captain of a huckleberry-party." Thoreau defended John Brown, writing "A Plea for Captain John Brown" and describing Brown's career as "meteor-like." Robert Richardson wrote biographies of both Thoreau (subtitled "A Life of the Mind") and Emerson.

Work: essay about a night in jail for refusing to pay the poll-tax; opens with "the best government is that which governs least"
Work: night in jail makes author realize neighbors' "friendship was for summer weather only"; calls the State "timid as a lone woman with her silver spoons"
Author: defended John Brown, writing "A Plea for Captain John Brown" and describing Brown's career as "meteor-like"
Author: Emerson's eulogy lamented that "instead of engineering for his entire country, he was the captain of a huckleberry-party"
